speaker: interviewer
text: I wanted to hear from the daughters how your mother's Holocaust experience affected your relationship with her and-- and with both your parents, since they're both survivors.

speaker: interviewee
text: You first?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We knew very little of my mother's experience growing up. More of my father's. But it wasn't until after we were married that--

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you think that was?

speaker: interviewee
text: It-- I don't-- I don't think my mother talked about it with anybody. Maybe a niece once. For a series of months, I think, she spoke about it. But we knew how-- you know, we-- we knew there were a lot of secrets. It was very difficult. And I remember as a child [SIGHS] looking through my mother's jewelry box, the way kids do. And there was a-- a cross. And I used to think, gee, maybe that's part of the secret. You know, maybe she's not really Jewish or-- I didn't-- I really didn't know the whole story. And then I may have asked her or it may have come out later, after Larry started to ask questions That the cross was actually a gift from the Polish family. That they continued to send money and clothing and gifts to-- you know, all the years following. And, you know, we knew they were-- we knew they were very, very dark secrets. But we didn't know what they were until, I guess-- I'm sure we were married. But we-- we don't remember when, exactly, Larry asked, because he just didn't-- he didn't really understand you know, that-- that the parents, the survivors, never really burden the children with-- with those kinds of stories. It was more you-- you knew that-- you knew they survived. You didn't know how, exactly. And I-- I think it was, you know, mostly to protect us. And it wasn't until much later that we realized. And-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I-- I agree. I think that, uh, everything that we knew about our parents' experiences is really from my father-- from my father. I-- we always heard those stories of what he went through, and where he went, and what happened, and just a little bit of what he told us about what happened with our mother. And we knew that she survived because she didn't look Jewish. And that was part of her whole experience. And-- but the-- all the details really didn't come out.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it's a really good question, why we didn't ask, you know? And my daughter asked us-- asked me, why didn't you ask? And I really don't know. I always felt it was something that they just wanted to be-- you know, they-- too-- too hard for her to really talk about. Though maybe she really wanted to talk about it. I-- it was too hard to know. I-- I think the whole atmosphere in the house was that you shouldn't push too hard on those kinds of things.

speaker: interviewee
text: But as far as what happened in the war generally and what happened to us right before we left Europe, we-- we knew all those stories, and all the connections between the people who survived, and survived here and survived in-- in Israel. And always very Zionistic, and always heard the stories of how my-- [CRYING] [PAUSES FOR 26 SECONDS] Well, anyway. I think everything that we get-- [CRYING] [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] All the good feelings that we have about being Jewish and about-- about loving Israel, and continuing to be Jewish is really from our parents-- that-- not only because of what they went through, but even before the war, how-- how really they came from such wonderful homes, and from such Zionistic homes. And it was really important to hear those stories, and even more than the Holocaust stories. I think those stories really made a wonderful impression on us, and really gave us something to pass along. And I'm always very proud of that. I always tell everybody about that. And I think that it's really-- it's really a shame, you know, that all that was lost, because it was a wonderful time before the war, with a-- a lovely town that wasn't so pure, that had its-- [INAUDIBLE] interesting characters. And I hear those stories also from my aunt, from my mother's sister. And she knows a lot further back than my mother. I mean, you know, she's much older than my mother. And it's just-- it's great to hear those stories. And I think that's-- it's wonderful that you're recording that, because it's not just the Holocaust. It's what was lost from before the Holocaust, how sad that is, because it was a wonderful time, and a wonderful time for Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I guess I-- I did ask a lot of questions. And there was one evening, when I was with Sheila and her parents. And I started asking so many, because I had never heard any of the story. And she started telling the story, and I think told maybe a lot of things that Sheila and even Sheila's dad maybe-- maybe didn't hear. I-- I come from a family that is not-- not too religious. And both my parents grew up in New York, and so didn't live through this. And I think for somebody like me, who was born after-- after the war, this whole thing really wasn't real. It just seemed too far away, too old. And the horrors were so unspeakable that it seemed like-- just unreal. And so for me joining this family, it made all of this real. And-- and I think that also is why this is so important. I think people grow up, it-- it seem it's history. And it seems so horrible that it's hard-- really hard to believe. So, you know, when we had children, and even though I wasn't brought up in a religious way, I felt like after what Irving and Sonia went through, I understood more, I think, the importance of preserving not just the religion, but also the culture. And-- and I also felt that, you know, it's the very least we could do, you know, for--

speaker: interviewee
text: To honor them.

speaker: interviewee
text: --for Irving and Sonia. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Just one more thing, and that is that I think, you know, after hearing their story-- I think it's incredible how great they are as people-- you know, as grandparents, parents, in-laws. I don't know if anybody could imagine how they would act having gone through what they went through. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Is there anybody else who'd like to add anything? Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you know, it started out that, like-- I mean, when I was, like, younger. I mean, I'm still young, but, you know, papa would always tell us stories about his experience in the Holocaust. And baba would just, you know, sit there and listen. And she would have to-- you know, we'd have to ask her if we wanted to know anything about what happened. And, you know, she-- papa always told us the stories. And I really-- I knew what happened, but-- and baba, I-- I never really knew what happened. I just knew, you know, like, basics like-- just, like, when she moved. She went here, and went there, and her mother sent her here, and whatever. And, you know, I really want to see this tape because I never really knew, like, any stories or anything like that. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewee
text: I just want to say that this tape is very important to me, because it's very important to me to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] be able to respond to what happened and to know what happened. And it's difficult because I'm so removed. I didn't grow up in a-- in a family that was the child-- a Holocaust family directly. And I'm really two generations away. But it's important for me to-- to have the information. And it's important for me to know what my grandmother experienced. And I'm very grateful that she's able to do this. And I hope that it is empowering to talk about it and not have it be a specter that is never discussed and is shameful. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I'm proud that she's able to [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] finally talk about her experiences. And I hope that she will be happy with her decision to do that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Like, at school, sometimes we talk about the Holocaust. And I've written report-- a report about papa, you know, talking about the Holocaust. And it's-- it's just so terrible, like, what happened and everything. But I'm so-- it's so great for people to know. And we should-- everybody-- I'm so happy that everybody's, you know, doing-- doing this kind of thing, because people have to know about what happened. And people can't forget.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Mrs. Sklaver, is there anything you'd like to say? Or is there anything that anybody else still wants to say? Avi?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jonathan [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe I'll finish. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll try to hold it together this time.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, I'm reading a book right now about the Holocaust. And, you know, just the stories are, like, really amazing about some of the kids, what they went through. And all I can think about what I'm reading is baba and papa [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and how it's so amazing how they survived. You know, both them being the youngest, how they made it through the tragedies and everything that happened and-- [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] add something.

speaker: interviewee
text: Avi wants to say something.

speaker: interviewer
text: Avi?

speaker: interviewee
text: Um, I-- I think that everybody in our family sees baba and papa as not only great grandparents and parents, but as very special people. And I think the fact that they survived the Holocaust makes them that much more special. They have all these experiences to share with us and advice to give us. And it makes their advice and what they have to say that much more valuable. And I think our family also has a lot of respect for our grandparents, not only for what they've been through, but for the values and the tradition that they have passed down through the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: On some level, one of the attractions that Linda had for me was that she was a daughter of survivors, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] because I felt that linking with her was linking to this [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] authentic [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] event in Jewish history and to the wonderful civilization that preceded it that Linda spoke about. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] as Linda said, I feel the same way that through her parents, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we-- we see not only their heroism in surviving the-- the event, but also, they mirror the wonderful civilization and the culture [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that flourished in Europe for so many generations and was so completely eradicated, but for these few survivors and for what they've brought and taught.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, to summarize the thing, I want to thank you, Spielberg, you and everybody, for coming out to listen to such powerful stories and to be patient [INAUDIBLE] it is wonderful. And I want to say thank you to my wife, Sonia, because she was so quiet, I never thought she would go to [INAUDIBLE] and undertake this kind of experience. I never think what she said I can [INAUDIBLE] she went through. That's-- even maybe it is the least. Maybe she had more. But after so many years until [INAUDIBLE] distinctly to say that I really appreciate it very-- I appreciate it very much to my family, to the children and grandchildren, to come out and spend some time in this thing. I want to thank you. And I hope it will never, never happen again to no nation, to the Jewish people, to anybody else.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much. Thank you all very, very much.
